name: Connection pool exhaustion from slow consumers
trigger_shapes: database connection pool with a fixed max-connections limit
HTTP connection pool or thread pool servicing downstream requests
connection acquired inside a try block without a guaranteed release path
pool size configured once at startup and never adjusted based on load
slow query or slow downstream causing connections to be held longer than expected
activation: A slow downstream (database, third-party API, or internal service) causes requests to hold connections longer than normal. The pool drains as in-flight requests accumulate. New requests cannot acquire a connection and queue or fail. The pool stays exhausted until the slow consumers complete — which may never happen if they are themselves waiting for connections.
mechanism: Request A acquires a connection; the query runs slowly (bad index, lock contention, or downstream latency spike); while A holds the connection, requests B through N arrive and each acquires a connection; pool reaches max_connections; request N+1 tries to acquire and blocks or raises a timeout exception; blocking requests pile up in thread pools; thread pool exhaustion follows connection pool exhaustion; the application becomes unresponsive even though the root cause was a single slow query.

signal_sequence.signal: Query latency p99 rises slightly. Connection wait times increase but connections are still granted. No connection pool metric is watched.
Connection acquisition timeout errors appear in logs. A subset of requests fail with 'could not acquire connection within timeout'. Pool utilisation metric (if it exists) shows > 90%.
All connections exhausted. New requests fail immediately. Thread pool fills with waiting requests. Application health checks begin failing. Dependent services start logging downstream errors.
Service returns 503 or 500 for all requests. A restart of the application temporarily recovers the service but the root cause (slow consumer) is still present.

threshold_estimated: Pool utilisation > 80% sustained for 60 seconds is the early warning. At > 95% the failure cascade is imminent. Based on common HikariCP and pgBouncer operational defaults (max_connections typically 10–50 for application pools).
invisible_because: Connection pool metrics are rarely in the primary dashboard. Developers see 'connection refused' or 'timeout' errors and assume a network issue. The root cause (slow query holding connections) is a level removed from the symptom (new requests failing). Load tests typically run fast queries against a fast database — pool exhaustion under realistic slow-query conditions is never exercised.
diagnostic: Is pool utilisation (active_connections / max_connections) tracked with an alert threshold? Under simulated slow-query load (add `pg_sleep(5)` to a representative query), does the application degrade gracefully or exhaust the pool within 30 seconds?
countermeasures: Set connection acquisition timeout (not just query timeout) — requests should fail fast rather than queue indefinitely.
Size the pool based on measured concurrency, not defaults: pool_size = (number_of_cores * 2) + effective_spindle_count is the HikariCP formula.
Alert on pool utilisation > 80% sustained, not just on connection errors (which fire too late).
Use a connection proxy (PgBouncer, ProxySQL) in transaction-mode pooling to share connections across many application threads.
Add query timeouts at the statement level — a runaway query should not hold a connection indefinitely.
In circuit breaker logic, treat 'pool exhausted' as a failure signal, not an infrastructure error, so it triggers fast-fail for callers.

sources.title: HikariCP — Pool Sizing Guidance (About Pool Sizing wiki)
